title: 1 2 Chain Rule
body:
1.2 THE CHAIN RULE

The Chain Rule for functions of more than one variable: If z = f (x, y) and x = x(t) and y = y(t) then

∂z ∂z ∂x ∂z ∂y = + ∂t ∂x ∂t ∂y ∂t

If z = f (x, y) and x = x(u, v) and y = y(u, v) then

∂z ∂z ∂x ∂z ∂y ∂z ∂z ∂x ∂z ∂y = + and = + ∂u ∂x ∂u ∂y ∂u ∂v ∂x ∂v ∂y ∂v

A common situation is that z is a function of x and y with x and y themselves functions of other variables, say u and v. Then z is also a function of u and v and it makes sense ∂z to ask the question “what is ?” ∂u The chain rule enables us to answer this question without actually ever having to calculate z as an explicit function of u and v. It is usually much more effective to use the chain rule than to calculate the new function w(u, v) = z (x(u, v), y(u, v)). Two version of the chain rule are shown above. However, all you need to remember is that you just apply the single-variable chain rule to each argument of z. dz Example 1 Given z = x2 y 3 with x = 2t and y = sin(4t), use the chain rule to find . dt Verify your answer by first substituting x(t) and y(t) into z(x, y).

F 8t sin3 (4t) + 48t2 sin2 (4t) cos(4t) F

∂z ∂z Example 2 Suppose z = x2 + 4y where x = u3 ln(v) and y = uv 2 . Find and . ∂u ∂v

∂z 5 2 2 ∂z 2u6 ln(v) F = 6u (ln(v)) + 4v , = + 8uv F ∂u ∂v v

Example 3 If z = a2 + b + c5 + d7 where a = uv, b = 2u + 3v, c = u2 and d = v 2 use ∂z the chain rule to find . ∂u

F 2uv 2 + 10u9 + 2 F

∂w Example 4 If w = a2 − ab3 with a = euv and b = 3u + 2v use the chain rule to find ∂u when u = 0 and v = 1.

F − 42 F

Example 5 Suppose that temperature in the plane is given by T (x, y) = x2 +y 2 . A particle is traveling along a path C defined parametrically by x = t − 1 y = t3 − 3t2 + 3t + 1 where t is time in seconds. Show that T (t) has a stationary point when t = 1.

Example 6 If w = f (u, v) with u = x + y and v = x − y show that

∂w ∂w ∂w ∂w = ( )2 − ( )2 ∂x ∂y ∂u ∂v
